content.thinking: Let me look at the chart image first to understand what it shows.

I can see the chart shows percent of total alcohol consumption by type (beer, spirits, wine, other) for different countries from 1965-2015. I need to scroll down to see more countries. Let me look at more of the image or crop different parts.

From what I can see so far:
- The legend shows: beer (orange/tan), spirits (blue), wine (red/pink), other (yellow?)
- Wait, looking more carefully: beer // spirits // wine // other
  - beer appears to be the tan/beige color


- spirits appears to be the blue color
- wine appears to be the red/pink color

Looking at the color patterns across the stacked area charts, I can identify that Chile and Austria both show notably large wine proportions compared to other countries. Let me zoom in on specific regions to get clearer details, particularly for Chile which stands out with its dominant wine section.
Let me get the full image dimensions and see all the countries. The image is 1970x1028 pixels. Let me crop to see Chile more clearly and also look at the full image to see all countries.
I need to see the full image. The image is 1970x1028. The chart has 2 rows visible (perhaps more rows below). Let me crop a wider view to see all countries.

Now I can see both rows:
- Row 1: Australia, Austria, Belgium, Brazil, Canada
- Row 2: Chile, China, Costa Rica, Cuba, Czechia

The chart seems to only show 2 rows total. Looking at the charts:

Legend: beer (tan/beige) // spirits (blue) // wine (red/pink) // other

Looking at each country:
- Chile: The chart is almost entirely red/pink (wine) and blue (spirits). The wine (red/pink) portion seems very large - it looks like wine dominates for most of the period, especially in earlier years.


- Austria: Also has a substantial red/pink (wine) portion
- Australia: Has some wine but less dominant

Chile's chart is particularly striking with wine consumption appearing to account for roughly 80-90% of the total in the 1960s-1970s, with the red/pink section dominating the entire visualization. Let me examine Chile's proportions more closely to get a better sense of how wine consumption has shifted over time.

Now I can see Chile's chart clearly. The pink/red area (wine) dominates significantly, especially in the early years. In the 1965-1980 period, wine seems to take up about 70-80% of total consumption. Even though it decreases over time, it's still a substantial portion.

Let me also look at other countries to compare. Let me look at Austria more carefully since it also seemed to have significant wine.

But wait, looking at the chart structure more carefully:
- The colors from top to bottom are: tan/beige (beer), blue (spirits), pink/red (wine), and there might be a "other" category at the very bottom


- In Chile's 1965 period, wine is taking up roughly three-quarters of the total consumption area.

I should check if there are additional countries displayed below this one.

I can see China's chart - it's mostly blue (spirits) with some tan/beige (beer) and very little pink/red (wine). This is definitely not the country with the highest wine percentage.

Let me look at Austria's chart in the first row.

I can see Austria's chart - it has a significant amount of beer (tan/beige) at the top, some blue (spirits) and a layer of pink/red (wine) at the bottom. The wine portion is probably around 20-30% for Austria.

Compared to Chile where wine is much larger (maybe 70-80% in early years), Chile clearly has a much higher wine proportion.

Let me verify by looking at the full chart comparison again.

From what I've seen:
- Chile has wine as the dominant drink category, especially in the 1965-1985 period, with the pink/red taking up about 70-80% of the chart area


- Austria, Australia, and China all have much smaller wine portions, with China showing almost no wine at all and relying heavily on spirits instead.

So Chile stands out as having by far the highest wine consumption relative to other alcohol types.

content.text:
Based on my analysis of the chart, Chile clearly stands out with the largest wine (pink/red) section, dominating especially in the 1965-1985 period.

<think>
The chart shows percent of total alcohol consumption by type (beer=tan, spirits=blue, wine=pink/red, other) for various countries from 1965-2015.

Chile's chart shows wine (pink/red) taking up approximately 70-80% of total alcohol consumption in the 1960s-1970s. While this proportion decreases over time, Chile's wine percentage is dramatically higher than any other country shown (Australia, Austria, Belgium, Brazil, Canada, China, Costa Rica, Cuba, Czechia).

Austria has some wine but beer dominates. China is overwhelmingly spirits. Most other countries are primarily beer or spirits-dominated.

Chile, as a major wine-producing country, clearly has the highest wine percentage in its total alcohol consumption.
</think>

<answer>
Chile has the highest percentage of wine in its total alcohol consumption. Its chart is dominated by the wine (pink/red) category, reaching approximately 70-80% of total alcohol consumption in the 1960s-1970s, far exceeding all other countries shown.
</answer>
